comment_text.sn22.55:1.3: I follow the interpretation established by Bhikkhu Bodhi, for which see his note 75 on this discourse in Connected Discourses. When using first person pronouns, the formula epitomizes the annihilationist view: “I might not be” (SN 22.81:11.9, SN 24.4:1.3, SN 22.153:1.3, AN 10.29:19.1). The Buddha reformed it with the impersonal third person pronoun: “It might not be”.
comment_text.sn22.55:8.9: “Will vanish” is vibhavissati, literally “will cease to be”, the verbal form of the word “nonexistence” (vibhava) in the second noble truth. It’s used throughout Snp 4.11 and, in a meditation context, in AN 11.9:7.1. At Iti 49:7.3 it refers to the ending of continued existence for an arahant.
comment_text.sn22.55:9.4: Here the monk quotes the Buddha, as indicated by the close -ti in the text.

root_text.sn22.55:1.3: “‘no cassaṁ, no ca me siyā, nābhavissa, na me bhavissatī’ti—
root_text.sn22.55:1.4: evaṁ adhimuccamāno bhikkhu chindeyya orambhāgiyāni saṁyojanānī”ti.
root_text.sn22.55:1.5: Evaṁ vutte, aññataro bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.55:1.6: “yathā kathaṁ pana, bhante, ‘no cassaṁ, no ca me siyā, nābhavissa, na me bhavissatī’ti—
root_text.sn22.55:1.7: evaṁ adhimuccamāno bhikkhu chindeyya orambhāgiyāni saṁyojanānī”ti?
root_text.sn22.55:2.2: sappurisadhamme avinīto rūpaṁ attato samanupassati, rūpavantaṁ vā attānaṁ; attani vā rūpaṁ, rūpasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.55:2.6: viññāṇaṁ attato samanupassati, viññāṇavantaṁ vā attānaṁ; attani vā viññāṇaṁ, viññāṇasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.55:3.1: So aniccaṁ rūpaṁ ‘aniccaṁ rūpan’ti yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti,
root_text.sn22.55:7.1: Sutavā ca kho, bhikkhu, ariyasāvako ariyānaṁ dassāvī ariyadhammassa kovido ariyadhamme suvinīto sappurisānaṁ dassāvī sappurisadhammassa kovido sappurisadhamme suvinīto
root_text.sn22.55:8.1: So aniccaṁ rūpaṁ ‘aniccaṁ rūpan’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti.
root_text.sn22.55:8.8: saṅkhataṁ rūpaṁ …pe… saṅkhataṁ viññāṇaṁ ‘saṅkhataṁ viññāṇan’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti.
root_text.sn22.55:9.1: So rūpassa vibhavā, vedanāya vibhavā, saññāya vibhavā, saṅkhārānaṁ vibhavā, viññāṇassa vibhavā, evaṁ kho, bhikkhu,
root_text.sn22.55:9.2: ‘no cassaṁ, no ca me siyā, nābhavissa, na me bhavissatī’ti—
root_text.sn22.55:9.3: evaṁ adhimuccamāno bhikkhu chindeyya orambhāgiyāni saṁyojanānī”ti.
root_text.sn22.55:10.1: Kathaṁ pana, bhante, jānato kathaṁ passato anantarā āsavānaṁ khayo hotī”ti?
root_text.sn22.55:10.2: “Idha, bhikkhu, assutavā puthujjano atasitāye ṭhāne tāsaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn22.55:10.4: ‘no cassaṁ, no ca me siyā, nābhavissa, na me bhavissatī’ti.
root_text.sn22.55:11.1: Sutavā ca kho, bhikkhu, ariyasāvako atasitāye ṭhāne na tāsaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn22.55:11.2: Na heso, bhikkhu, tāso sutavato ariyasāvakassa:
root_text.sn22.55:11.3: ‘no cassaṁ, no ca me siyā, nābhavissa, na me bhavissatī’ti.
root_text.sn22.55:11.4: Rūpupayaṁ vā, bhikkhu, viññāṇaṁ tiṭṭhamānaṁ tiṭṭheyya, rūpārammaṇaṁ rūpappatiṭṭhaṁ nandūpasecanaṁ vuddhiṁ virūḷhiṁ vepullaṁ āpajjeyya.
root_text.sn22.55:11.7: saṅkhārupayaṁ vā, bhikkhu, viññāṇaṁ tiṭṭhamānaṁ tiṭṭheyya, saṅkhārārammaṇaṁ saṅkhārappatiṭṭhaṁ nandūpasecanaṁ vuddhiṁ virūḷhiṁ vepullaṁ āpajjeyya.
root_text.sn22.55:12.2: ‘ahamaññatra rūpā, aññatra vedanāya, aññatra saññāya, aññatra saṅkhārehi viññāṇassa āgatiṁ vā gatiṁ vā cutiṁ vā upapattiṁ vā vuddhiṁ vā virūḷhiṁ vā vepullaṁ vā paññāpessāmī’ti, netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn22.55:13.1: Rūpadhātuyā ce, bhikkhu, bhikkhuno rāgo pahīno hoti. Rāgassa pahānā vocchijjatārammaṇaṁ patiṭṭhā viññāṇassa na hoti.
root_text.sn22.55:13.5: viññāṇadhātuyā ce, bhikkhu, bhikkhuno rāgo pahīno hoti. Rāgassa pahānā vocchijjatārammaṇaṁ patiṭṭhā viññāṇassa na hoti.
root_text.sn22.55:13.7: Vimuttattā ṭhitaṁ. Ṭhitattā santusitaṁ. Santusitattā na paritassati. Aparitassaṁ paccattaññeva parinibbāyati.
root_text.sn22.55:13.9: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhu, jānato evaṁ passato anantarā āsavānaṁ khayo hotī”ti.

translation_text.sn22.55:1.2: There the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
translation_text.sn22.55:1.3: “‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine.’
translation_text.sn22.55:1.4: A mendicant who makes such a resolution can cut off the lower fetters.”
translation_text.sn22.55:1.5: When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha,
translation_text.sn22.55:1.7: can a mendicant who makes such a resolution cut off the lower fetters?”
translation_text.sn22.55:2.1: “Mendicant, take an unlearned ordinary person who has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in their teaching. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in their teaching.
translation_text.sn22.55:2.2: They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form.
translation_text.sn22.55:2.6: consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.55:3.1: They don’t truly understand form—which is impermanent—as impermanent.
translation_text.sn22.55:4.1: They don’t truly understand form—which is suffering—as suffering.
translation_text.sn22.55:5.1: They don’t truly understand form—which is not-self—as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.55:6.1: They don’t truly understand form—which is conditioned—as conditioned.
translation_text.sn22.55:6.6: They don’t truly understand that form will vanish.
translation_text.sn22.55:6.7: They don’t truly understand that feeling …
translation_text.sn22.55:7.1: But a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons.
translation_text.sn22.55:7.2: They don’t regard form as self …
translation_text.sn22.55:8.1: They truly understand form—which is impermanent—as impermanent.
translation_text.sn22.55:8.6: They truly understand form … feeling … perception … choices … consciousness—which is suffering—as suffering.
translation_text.sn22.55:8.7: They truly understand form … feeling … perception … choices … consciousness—which is not-self—as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.55:8.8: They truly understand form … feeling … perception … choices … consciousness—which is conditioned—as conditioned.
translation_text.sn22.55:8.9: They truly understand that form will vanish.
translation_text.sn22.55:9.1: It’s because of the vanishing of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness that a mendicant who makes such a resolution—
translation_text.sn22.55:9.2: ‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine’—
translation_text.sn22.55:9.3: can cut off the lower fetters.”
translation_text.sn22.55:9.4: “Sir, you say that a mendicant who makes such a resolution can cut off the lower fetters.
translation_text.sn22.55:10.1: But how are they to know and see in order to end the defilements without delay?”
translation_text.sn22.55:10.2: “Mendicant, an unlearned ordinary person worries about things that aren’t a worry.
translation_text.sn22.55:10.3: For an unlearned ordinary person worries:
translation_text.sn22.55:10.4: ‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine.’
translation_text.sn22.55:11.1: A learned noble disciple doesn’t worry about things that aren’t a worry.
translation_text.sn22.55:11.2: For a learned noble disciple doesn’t worry:
translation_text.sn22.55:11.3: ‘It might not be, and it might not be mine. It will not be, and it will not be mine.’
translation_text.sn22.55:11.4: As long as consciousness continues, it would get involved with form, supported by form, established on form. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature.
translation_text.sn22.55:11.5: Or consciousness would get involved with feeling …
translation_text.sn22.55:11.6: Or consciousness would get involved with perception …
translation_text.sn22.55:11.7: Or consciousness would get involved with choices, supported by choices, grounded on choices. And with a sprinkle of relishing, it would grow, increase, and mature.
translation_text.sn22.55:12.1: Suppose, mendicant, you were to say:
translation_text.sn22.55:12.2: ‘Apart from form, feeling, perception, and choices, I will describe the coming and going of consciousness, its passing away and reappearing, its growth, increase, and maturity.’ That is not possible.
translation_text.sn22.55:13.1: If a mendicant has given up greed for the form element, the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.55:13.2: If a mendicant has given up greed for the feeling element …
translation_text.sn22.55:13.5: consciousness element, the support is cut off, and there is no establishment of consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.55:13.6: Since that consciousness does not become established and does not grow, not making choices, it is freed.
translation_text.sn22.55:13.7: Being free, it’s stable. Being stable, it’s content. Being content, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished.
translation_text.sn22.55:13.8: They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended … there is nothing further for this place.’
translation_text.sn22.55:13.9: The ending of the defilements is for one who knows and sees this.”
